summary: CLI uses anti-detection tradecraft (process impersonation, scheduled-task persistence, TLS-verification disable) to evade exam-proctoring software
details:
idlidosa is a CLI/Electron tool that markets itself as bypassing exam-proctoring and lockdown software. It does not exfiltrate installer data, run attacker-controlled remote code, or distribute third-party credentials, but it ships several malware-shaped techniques that fire when the operator runs `idlidosa start` (not on install or require):

- Process impersonation: `disguiseElectron()` in dist/cli/index.js copies the Electron binary to `msedgewebview2.exe` and uses rcedit to overwrite its version metadata to claim it is `Microsoft Edge WebView2 Runtime` from `Microsoft Corporation`, then launches the daemon under that forged identity.
- Persistence with masquerading name: `installResurrector()` drops `launch.json` and `resurrect.js` under `%APPDATA%/Idlidosa` and creates a Windows scheduled task named `MicrosoftEdgeWebView2Update` running every minute. The watchdog (cli/guard.cjs) sets `process.title = "Windows Audio Device Graph Isolation"` and restarts the daemon within ~1.5 seconds, with a code comment noting this is `fast enough to beat TestPad's 30s scan`.
- Globally disables TLS certificate verification: dist/shared/index.js sets `process.env.NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED = "0"` as a top-level side effect on module load, weakening every HTTPS request the daemon makes (including the Groq API key and screenshot payloads) on any network the user runs on.
- Bundled pool of AES-encrypted Groq API keys (`shared/keys.json`, 9 entries) with the decryption key derived from the hardcoded constant `pageai-pool-v2`, so any installer can extract and use them. These keys belong to the author's own Groq account — author self-harm, not third-party credential distribution.

The harmful behaviors do NOT trigger on `npm install` or on `require()`; they fire only when the operator explicitly invokes the bin (`idlidosa start`). The operator is also the consumer, so this is not a supply-chain attack against an unwitting installer. However, the persistence + impersonation + TLS-disable combination is malware-shaped and intended to evade legitimate security/proctoring tools on the user's own machine, which warrants human policy adjudication rather than auto-allow.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
